message:
So I was making dinner last week, pan on the stove, and my phone died right in the middle of a recipe video I was following. Totally fine—I just grabbed my old printed cookbook. But it hit me: what if it had been something more urgent, like checking my family's meetup spot after an evacuation, or making sure everyone got out okay?

That made me think about how much we rely on our phones for *everything* now, especially in emergencies. Which is exactly why the basics matter so much—the things that don't need batteries or WiFi. Working smoke alarms. A physical escape plan everyone knows. Knowing your exits by heart, not by GPS.

I'm not saying ditch your phone (mine's charging as I type this 😄), just that those old-school safety habits are kind of like having a backup. They've got your back when tech inevitably doesn't.

Do you keep a printed copy of your family's emergency plan anywhere, or is it mostly on your phone?
